source: Elevated RDW can indicate increased red blood cell production variability, which can be seen in inflammatory conditions like Crohn's disease.
Anemia (low hemoglobin) is a common finding in Crohn's disease due to blood loss from intestinal inflammation and malabsorption.
Anemia (low hematocrit) is a common finding in Crohn's disease due to blood loss from intestinal inflammation and malabsorption.
Elevated white blood cell count (leukocytosis) can indicate inflammation or infection, which can be associated with Crohn's disease.
Thrombocytopenia (low platelet count) can occur in Crohn's disease due to splenic sequestration, bone marrow suppression, or bleeding.
Microcytic anemia (low MCV) can be seen in Crohn's disease due to iron deficiency from blood loss or malabsorption.
Low mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) is associated with microcytic anemia, which can be seen in Crohn's disease due to iron deficiency from blood loss or malabsorption.
Low mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) is associated with microcytic anemia, which can be seen in Crohn's disease due to iron deficiency from blood loss or malabsorption.
Anemia (low red blood cell count) is a common finding in Crohn's disease due to blood loss from intestinal inflammation and malabsorption.
